retrieved_docs.title: Tracheostomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Panfacial trauma or craniofacial dislocation may contraindicate nasal intubation. Although alternative airway strategies should be attempted when feasible, the surgical team must be prepared to perform an emergent tracheostomy immediately in life-threatening cases, including laryngeal disruption or other acute airway compromise. [2] Elective tracheostomy is indicated in patients with prolonged mechanical ventilation, anticipated airway obstruction due to extensive head and neck surgery or edema (eg, from cancer, trauma, or radiation), obstructive sleep apnea refractory to other therapies, recurrent aspiration from impaired airway protection, neuromuscular disease with respiratory failure, and subglottic stenosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Cribriform Plate Fractures -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Nasotracheal intubation and nasogastric tube placement are contraindicated in patients with midface trauma due to the possibility of tubes being passed into the cranial vault via a fractured cribriform plate.

retrieved_docs.title: Tracheostomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Tracheostomy may be performed emergently or electively, depending on the clinical context. Emergent tracheostomy is warranted when less invasive airway access fails. Common scenarios include acute upper airway obstruction from foreign body aspiration, angioedema, deep neck infection such as Ludwig angina, and anaphylaxis. Other indications include formalization of a cricothyrotomy after airway stabilization, penetrating laryngeal trauma, and severe facial injuries such as LeFort III fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Maxillary Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The acute management of the patient is vital, and this may require the resuscitation team, including a cricothyroidotomy or tracheostomy to secure the airway. Midface fractures are a relative contraindication to nasal intubation, and these patients will eventually need an airway that does not go between the teeth to allow for reconstruction. However, with glide scopes and fiberoptic scopes, some anesthesia providers are comfortable nasally intubating these patients. Another option is a submental intubation when done with a surgical team. Essentially, the patient is orally intubated, an incision is made under the chin in the submental area, and the tube pulled through.

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Fractures -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Orotracheal intubation is typically preferred, although this can be very challenging in upper airway hemorrhage. Video laryngoscopy may help treat airway edema or injury; this method is ineffective if there is significant bleeding due to obstruction of the camera lens. A surgical airway, such as a tracheostomy or cricothyroidotomy, may be required if orotracheal intubation is impractical due to obstruction from anatomical distortion, edema, or hemorrhage. The risk of life-threatening hemorrhage in Le Fort II and III injuries is higher than that associated with other facial injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Care Principles -- Function -- Airway
retrieved_docs.content: Look: at the patient’s face for micrognathia, maxillofacial trauma, a small oral opening, or any apparent external signs that may make intubating difficult.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Injury -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngotracheal injuries are serious, have high mortality, and require airway management Airway compromise may be delayed Tracheostomy is the preferred airway Stable injuries may need additional imaging such as CT scan Unstable injuries need operative management Do not delay surgical consultation [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Care Principles -- Function -- Airway
retrieved_docs.content: Neck: patients with short necks, those that have a cervical collar placed, or patients that have limited movement of the cervical spine, such as those with cervical osteoarthritis or fused cervical vertebrae, can impair the provider’s ability to manipulate the airway for endotracheal tube placement.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasal Fracture Reduction -- Indications -- History
retrieved_docs.content: In the acute phase, the simple application of ice and analgesia may be very helpful for reducing edema and discomfort. More severe facial trauma may require assessment and stabilization of the airway, using appropriate Advanced Trauma Life Support or Pediatric Advanced Life Support protocols.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Assessment -- Technique or Treatment -- Airway
retrieved_docs.content: Airway obstruction is a major, preventable cause of death in trauma victims, and therefore it is the initial step of the primary survey. [3] Assessing the conscious patient's airway starts with talking to the patient. Ask the patient their name to see if they respond clearly and appropriately. This will help assess the patency of the airway. Airway evaluation also includes a visual inspection of the patient. Look for signs of respiratory distress, listen for stridor, inspect the face, oral cavity, and neck, as well as palpate the patient's neck and face. When inspecting and palpating the patient, look for oral or dental injury, obstructions to intubation, such as unstable midface fractures, and even location for possible cricothyrotomy. If the patient is unconscious or not protecting their airway, they should be intubated immediately. If unable to intubate, cricothyrotomy should be performed. If intubating cervical spine immobilization must be maintained. If the patient requires intubation, be sure that the endotracheal tube remains secure as accidental extubation is a leading cause of morbidity in trauma patients. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Polytraumatized Patient -- Issues of Concern -- Airway Management (including cervical spine protection)
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical cricothyroidotomy involves a skin incision over and through the cricothyroid membrane to directly visualize the trachea and insert an endotracheal tube. Both needle and surgical cricothyroidotomy should only be performed by users who are familiar with the technique required for their success.

retrieved_docs.content: Class 1- full uvular visualization Class 2- the tongue occludes the distal portion of the uvula Class 3- the entire uvula is occluded except for the base or proximal portion Class 4- the uvula is unable to be visualized

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Securing the airway is the primary goal of early management. Intubation can be difficult and might lead to losing the airway if not performed with caution. Tracheostomy under local anesthesia is recommended if airway obstruction is severe. [22] [24] management of laryngeal trauma based on Schaefer–Fuhrman classification can categorize according to the following [15] : Nonsurgical/conservative (minor edema, hematoma, and mucosal laceration): Observation, delivery of humidified air, voice rest for group 1 Surgical options: group 2 through 5, which includes endoscopy, endoscopy with exploration, and endoscopy with exploration with stenting

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Care Principles -- Function -- Airway
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluate 3-3-2: First, while approximating the patient’s finger width, they should be able to accommodate three fingers stacked vertically between the midline of their front teeth with their mouth fully opened. Second, they should be able to stack three fingers horizontally between their chin and their hyoid bone. Finally, they should be able to stack two fingers between the thyroid cartilage and the floor of their mouth. If all three criteria are met, the provider can anticipate that the patient's anatomy will allow for easier intubation. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea serves as the anatomical site for tracheostomy procedures, and these procedures may be performed as either temporary or permanent interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2160
retrieved_docs.content: Tracheoscopy via tracheostomy is performed directly on the trachea, making the trachea the direct procedural site for this endoscopic examination. In contrast, tracheostomy—whether performed as a temporary or permanent airway access—affects the trachea indirectly, serving as an indirect procedural site. Understanding the distinction between direct and indirect procedural involvement of the trachea helps clinicians select appropriate techniques for airway management and diagnostic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea is directly linked to the procedure denoted by تنبيب الرغامى (tracheal intubation). In addition, the trachea serves as an indirect procedural site for tracheostomy, which may be performed as either a temporary or permanent intervention. Furthermore, operations involving the trachea and bronchus have a broader relationship with تنبيب الرغامى, indicating that intubation is a common element across a range of airway surgeries. This network of relationships underscores the central role of the trachea in airway management and related surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is classified as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, indicating that this procedure involves the insertion of an oral and pharyngeal airway to establish and maintain a secure airway for assisted ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion of a tracheal T‑tube is carried out by employing the technique of التنبيب (intubation), and the process of التنبيب itself encompasses the specific method of تركيب أنبوب فغر الرغامى (placement of a tracheal T‑tube). This relationship highlights that the procedural step of inserting a tracheal T‑tube relies on intubation as its underlying method, while intubation is defined in part by the act of installing the tracheal T‑tube. Understanding this linkage is important for clinicians to correctly apply the appropriate technique when managing airway interventions that require a tracheal T‑tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2131
retrieved_docs.content: Tracheoscopy via tracheostomy is regarded as equivalent to other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum, representing a specific method of visualizing the trachea through a tracheostomy that falls within the broader category of respiratory and mediastinal diagnostic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1735
retrieved_docs.content: Trachea and bronchus operations represent a broad category of surgical and procedural interventions involving the airway, and within this category the specific procedure of تنبيب الرغامى (tracheal intubation) is included.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1893
retrieved_docs.content: Implantation procedure is classified under the broader category of التنبيب (intubation). The Insertion of catheter into esophagus, permanent tube type (procedure) represents a more specific, narrower form of التنبيب, indicating a specialized application of intubation techniques. Transglottic catheterization of trachea employs التنبيب as its procedural method, illustrating that this airway access technique is performed through intubation. Together, these relationships highlight how various catheter‑based interventions are organized under the overarching concept of التنبيب, with some procedures serving as parent categories, others as specialized subtypes, and certain techniques directly utilizing intubation as their operative method.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1272
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the larynx and trachea are closely linked to the larynx and are classified as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum. In the broader procedural taxonomy, the larynx functions as an indirect procedure site for various non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Additionally, tracheoscopy via tracheostomy is regarded as synonymous with the category of other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum, reflecting its role within this diagnostic group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.
